data.title: Hilton Worldwide: Hotel Count Growth Is In The Price After A Mixed Q3 (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Hilton Worldwide (NYSE:HLT) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining 57%; however, shares did decline by over 2% on Wednesday in response to Q3 earnings and disappointing guidance. I last covered HLT in October 2023, rating shares a “buy,” and since then, the stock has gained 54%, 20% ahead of the S&P 500. Given such strong performance and updated financials, now is a good time to revisit Hilton.

In the company’s third quarter, Hilton earned $1.92, beating consensus by $0.08 even as revenue of $2.87 billion was $40 million below consensus. HLT generated $904 million in adjusted EBITDA, up 8.4% from last year, and it expanded margins to a robust 72.2% adjusted EBITDA margin, up 140bps from last year. Because of its comparatively fixed operating costs, G&A expenses rose by a more modest 5% to $101 million.

Hilton generates such tremendous margins because it is really a brand company, franchising out the hotels. Most of the hotels and associated real estate are owned by its franchisees, leaving Hilton as an exceedingly capital-light business. Indeed, it spent just $57 million in cap-ex last quarter. The key thing for Hilton is seeing growing results in its franchised hotels and expanding its franchise base.

Looking at its results, revenue per available room (RevPAR) is the key metric of a hotel’s productivity, and it rose by 1.4% on a constant currency basis to $121.40. Occupancy rose by 30bps to 75.3%. As you can see below, Europe was the standout, up 2.3% to 81.3% while Asia was the laggard, down 50bps to 73.2%.

Europe took a bit longer to recovery from COVID than the US, but it has fully caught up. Asia Pacific has been weighed down by weak results in China. China was down 9% while the rest of Asia was up 4%. It is seeing little international travel into China. Management expects China to be better next year given stimulus. Government spending efforts should help to arrest the deterioration in this critical market, but I am cautious as to the timing and magnitude of the recovery, particularly given ongoing US-China tensions, which seems to have weighed on US brands in the Chinese market.

Interestingly, there has been a bit of divergence among Hilton’s brands. Hilton’s two upscale brands, Waldorf Astoria and Curio, reported over 4% gains in RevPAR, the strongest of any brands. Its lower end Hampton and Homewood Suites were flat over the past year. This argues that higher-end consumers are holding up better than lower-end consumers, which makes sense as the cumulative impact of inflation over the past three years has likely done more to wipe out excess savings of those with lower incomes.

Indeed, as you can see below, after steadily rising post-pandemic, spending on travel has stalled out over the past few quarters as consumers’ discretionary budgets come under pressure. In an acknowledgement of this, Hilton trimmed guidance. It expects RevPAR to be up 2-2.5% this year, down from 2-3% previously. It continues to expect $6.93-$7.03 in EPS. These revisions are driven by a slowing in Q4 with RevPAR up just 1-2%, and adjusted EBITDA declining by at least $70 million sequentially. As such, it expects $1.57-$1.67 in adjusted EPS well below the $1.74 consensus for Q4.

While per-hotel growth is slowing, Hilton has benefited from a significant expansion in its operations. It opened 531 hotels with 36,600 rooms in Q3, a company record. Accordingly, unit growth was 7.8% from last year, a record. This increase in the unit count combined with modest RevPAR growth allowed management and franchise fee revenues to rise by 8.3%.

Hilton now has 8,200 hotels in its system, and after Q3, it now has over 700 hotels in China. This has become a critical market for the company, and until we see China return to growth, overall growth is likely to be constrained.

Management expects growth in 2025 to be similar to 2024. It expects to see Europe and the Middle East slow but remain the best region next year while the pace of decline in China should be smaller given easier comparisons. These factors should broadly offset. Absent a recession (which is not my base case), activity in the US should be somewhat positive. I think it is important to note that even with its rebound, travel spending is a smaller share of consumption than pre-pandemic.

In other words, consumers are not “over-spending” on travel even as it seems like the sector has boomed. Rather, the level is fairly normal with arguably some room for growth. As such, I expect travel spending to be at least as fast as consumption over the next year, though the deceleration in RevPAR trends does point to ongoing divergence by income-level.

The bigger driver of Hilton’s growth is going to be its expansion efforts. It has 492k room in its development pipeline across 3,525 hotels, up 8% from last year. These efforts will grow its hotel count by over 40%. Importantly, its franchisees face most of the cost of this expansion, as evidenced by HLT’s minimal cap-ex needs. This pipeline will take several years to complete, but 48% of its pipeline is under construction.

With ~1,700 hotels under construction, HLT is poised to grow its hotel count by 6+% per year (or at least 500 hotels) over each of the next 3-5 years. I would also note that about 57% of its development pipeline is outside of the US as it continues to expand its footprint and enter higher growth markets where it has less saturation.

With its cash flow from franchise fees, Hilton has been aggressively returning capital to shareholders. It has done $2.4 billion of capital return so far this year and should end 2024 at about $3 billion. This has resulted in a 5% decline in the share count over the past year. Due in part to capital returns, its debt load has risen from $9.2 billion to $11.3 billion in debt. As such, interest expense rose to $140 million from $113 million last year.

Still, its balance sheet is healthy given the recurring nature of its cash flows. It has just 2.8x net debt to EBITDA, and only $500 million in debt matures before 2027. With $1.65 billion in cash, it can pay that maturity out of cash. As such, Hilton does not have much exposure to higher interest rates as rates could be low again by the time it needs to refinance debt.

Hilton’s aggressive hotel count growth should enable solid earnings growth to continue; however, Q3 results do speak to slower growth in its existing business. Still, with ~6% hotel count growth and 0-2% RevPAR growth, HLT should be able to grow revenue by about 7% per year and earnings by 10-12% over 2025-2027. Combined with ~3-5% share count reduction, EPS should rise by about 15%.

Shares are expensive at about 33x 2024 EPS because markets see this credible growth runway given how much of its pipeline is already under construction. Based on this trajectory, HLT could grow EPS by over 50% by 2027 to ~$11, absent a recession or further downturn in China. At a forward multiple of 25x reflecting its high-margin, recurring cash flow, HLT could trade towards $275 by the end of 2026.

That is about 18% upside over the next 26 months or about 8% per year. That is a market-like return, and it still assumes a fairly hefty forward multiple. In other words, Hilton shares appear to be fully discounting the significant growth the business may experience. With all of that potential good news in the price, any weakness in the economy will create downside, and it is difficult to see even greater upside catalysts.

As such, I am moving shares to a hold after Q3 results. Given mid-teens EPS growth, I do not see a need to sell or an argument to short the stock, absent a significant economic downturn. However, valuation is now full, and investors should look elsewhere for better buy opportunities.

data.title: BBB Foods Inc.: Buy The Growth
data.description:
BBB Foods Inc. (NYSE:TBBB) is a Mexican discounted grocery chain with over 2,500 stores across 15 States in Mexico. The company, BBB Foods Inc., is an acronym for "Bueno, Bonito y Barato", which translates to "Good, Nice and Affordable". In the past 5 years, the company has grown significantly and this has resulted in its share price rising by more than 50% in less than a year.

I believe this growth will only continue and improve.

The company so far has been on a strong growth trajectory. The growth story continues as the company expands its store count while maintaining high profit margins and increasing same-store sales.

BBB Foods Inc. Has Strong Historic Growth

Since 2019, the company has enjoyed same-store sales growth of over 10% annually. In its worst year during COVID in 2021, it averaged just 12.3%:

(Source: BBB Foods Inc. Financials)

Similarly, its store count has grown by ~20.2%:

(Source: BBB Foods Inc. Financials)

While its sales and stores are growing, it's keeping pace by building new distribution centers to keep its supply chain operating efficiently. BBB Foods Inc. has been building at least 1 new distribution center a year:

(Source: BBB Foods Inc. Financials)

To put things into perspective, distribution centers are a large capital outlay and companies typically do not build them unless it is to lay the groundwork for large growth.

BBB Foods Inc. Competitive Advantage

BBB Foods Inc. is a discounted dollar store and its operating strategy is focused on 4 areas:

High turnaround of its 800 products

BBB Foods Inc. has a limited selection of products and this allows the retailer to purchase a narrow selection of products in bulk at volume discounts, and favorable terms with suppliers.

Strong private label

BBB Foods Inc. owns 95 different private labels and this represents more than 400 of its products offerings. Having private labels has allowed the retailer to achieve cost savings, which are transferred to the shoppers, and the price points are much more competitive than its competition.

Outsourcing the manufacturing to a wide variety of local manufacturers

BBB Foods Inc. doesn't manufacture any of its products. Instead, it relies on a network of over 100 local suppliers to make its private label. This has kept its supply chain nimble and less reliant on one domain supplier.

Good value for money

The company's pursuit of efficiency in its supply chain and cost savings has allowed it to deliver value to consumers. Its sales have grown immensely and more than doubled since 2021 (more on this below).

IPO Has Improved BBB Foods Inc. Balance Sheet

In applying the Piotroski F-Score to analyze BBB Foods Inc. financials, its financials score a 6 to 7 in its most recent quarterly results and a score of 5 in its year ending 2023. A Piotroski F-Score of 9 shows the company's financial statements are strong while a score of 0 implies the company's financials are weak:

(Source: BBB Foods Inc. Financials)

Note, in Q1-2024 and Q2-2024, BBB Foods Inc. had not disclosed its shares outstanding, so no additional points were given on the F-Score in those periods.

Before its Feb 2024 IPO, the company was highly leveraged at 82% in 2022 and 71% in 2023. This was a concern for a company that is in high growth mode. However, its IPO in Feb. 2024 has changed that, and new equity has led to fresh cash injected into the business. Currently, its long-term debt is hovering in the mid-30%.

Its current ratio has also benefited from the IPO as its current ratio is now closer to 0.80 from 0.50 in 2022 and 2023.

In looking at its profitability, BBB Foods Inc. does have improving profitability margins. Its gross margins have improved slightly from 15% in 2021 to 17%. This percentage may seem low, but for a discounted retailer like BBB Foods Inc., it does demonstrate management's ability to find efficiencies in its supply chain.

With 2 quarters of 2024 financials now available, it does appear BBB Foods Inc. is on track to beat its 2023 net income. However, this could also be a seasonal business, so there could be fluctuations in its sales that I haven't seen yet in Q3-2024 and Q4-2024.

Summarizing all the financial figures together, I believe BBB Foods Inc. has some fixed costs in its business to overcome. Since 2019, it has doubled its warehouses across Mexico, and these are capital-intensive investments. This explains why the retailer had taken on high levels of debt prior to its IPO. To offset some of these high costs, BBB Foods Inc. has been growing its store count aggressively, and its store count has - in fact - doubled from 2019.

Also, its same-store sales growth has been growing by more than 10% each year since 2020, and with its gross margins improving slightly, this implies the company has achieved a certain level of economies of scale.

Risk: BBB Foods Inc. Is not Consistently Profitable

The downside to this buy thesis is its profit inconsistency. The company does appear to be putting growth first over profit at the moment. The path to profitability also seems certain with its improving margins. However, I do caution potential investors here because it is an element of risk. There is ample cash and leverage flexibility for BBB Foods Inc. to spend its way out of trouble, but this is not a completely safe investment.

Conclusion: BBB Foods Inc. is a Buy

Given its recent IPO early this year, I do believe not many investors are aware of this company. The past 6 months have propelled this stock price upwards, and I believe there is further roadway for it to go up.

Another reason why investors should consider this stock is it operates in Mexico so for investors who are heavily invested in North America, this is a geographic diversification play.

Mexico's GDP growth has slowed significantly. Year-over-year growth slowed to 1.0% from 1.8% the prior quarter. What this means is a lot of shoppers are probably watching their wallets closely and are likely to be price-sensitive when they grocery shop. This plays well into BBB Foods Inc.'s value proposition as a discounted supermarket.

Overall, I am bullish on BBB Foods Inc.

data.title: CARY: Strong Investment-Grade Bond ETF, Above-Average 6.2% Yield, Outstanding Risk-Adjusted Returns
data.description:
I've been discussing the Angel Oak Income ETF (NYSEARCA:CARY), a small investment-grade bond ETF, in my Investment Group throughout the year. The reason is simple: CARY has become one of my top income ETFs, due to its above-average 6.2% yield, strong performance track record, and below-average risk and volatility. Overall, risk returns are sky-high, much higher than average. CARY has a few downsides too, at least significant ones. Lots of higher-yielding CEFs and ETFs should outperform the fund long-term, but for investors looking for high-quality investments, CARY is one of the best choices out there and a buy.

CARY - Overview and Investment Thesis

Strategy and Portfolio

CARY is an actively managed ETF focusing on short-term investment-grade securities, mostly non-agency MBS and similar investments. Asset allocations are as follows:

CARY is actively managed, so asset allocations and security selection are partly active investment decisions taken by its management team. CARY chose to be overweight assorted MBS products, due to some combination of higher expected returns, income, or lower risk. Let's take a closer look at what/why this is the case, I think it's interesting, and don't want to get too repetitive in my articles. CARY's management argues that:

We believe any easing cycle will support lower interest rate volatility, which should in turn support the outperformance of mortgages and securitized credit versus corporate credit after they lagged in 2023 due to elevated interest rate volatility.

The logic behind the above is, well, complicated. Agency mortgages can be thought of as treasuries, long-term securities backed by the U.S. Federal Government, with prepayment risk, one can pay their mortgage early without penalties, unlike most bonds. Said prepayment risk can be modeled as an option (the option to pay back your mortgage without penalty). Option prices increase with volatility. In this case, investors would demand comparatively higher yields for mortgages versus treasuries, due to the possibility of significant rate cuts (would cause mortgages to be refinanced lower, treasuries would maintain their coupons). Lower interest rate volatility should lead to lower MBS rates/spreads, insofar as this is correlated with fewer cuts.

The Federal Reserve argues the same, and has a nifty graph linking mortgage spreads with rate volatility:

Although I understand the argument, can't say I agree with it, at least not meaningfully. The relationship here seems weak, indirect, and volatile. At first glance, the graph above does seem to show a relationship between spreads and volatility, but the situation wasn't so obvious as I analyzed it further. One can see how spreads have increased since mid-2022 even as volatility is flat, for instance. Wider mortgage spreads are due to the possibility of rate cuts, which can be construed as interest rate volatility, but these do seem like somewhat different phenomena.

In my opinion, MBS looks attractive because mortgage spreads are at historically above-average levels. These have rarely been higher, excepting a couple of recessions and the past year or two.

Higher mortgage spreads higher MBS spreads, a straightforward benefit for investors in these securities, including CARY. Simpler, more straightforward argument compared to interest rate volatility.

In any case, both CARY's management and I are bullish on MBS, although for different reasons.

Decisions such as outweighing MBS are meant to increase returns and income, and/or decrease risk and volatility. This specific decision has led to higher returns and outperformance, as per the fund's latest annual report. I've seen similar positions, and outperformance, in other investment-grade ETFs too.

Active funds make decisions such as the above constantly, index funds rarely do, simply blindly tracking an index (which also rarely makes decisions). Choosing an active fund means betting on the fund's management team, and on the success and effectiveness of these decisions. Index funds are simpler and somewhat less risky, but potential returns and income are both lower. I'm a big believer of active bond funds, with these having reasonable performance track records, much stronger than active equity.

As can be seen above, around half of the active bond funds in the market outperform the index, at least the cheaper ones. In my opinion, with some analysis and a small edge, one can achieve higher success rates, leading to somewhat consistent long-term outperformance. CARY definitely has, which leads me to my next point.

Outstanding Risk-Return Profile

CARY's overall risk-return profile is outstanding, with above-average income and returns, and below-average risk and volatility.

CARY's 6.2% dividend yield is higher than that of most bonds, only slightly lower than high-yield corporate bonds.

CARY's returns are quite strong too, and much more stable than average:

Both credit and rate risk are low, with the fund focusing on short-term, investment-grade securities:

Sharpe and Sortino ratios are sky-high, both since inception:

And for the past year:

Do remember that CARY's overall portfolio and strategy is somewhat conservative, with diversified high-quality holdings, focusing on MBS. There are no significant risks or negatives here, from either its overall portfolio, strategy, credit quality, rate risk, or other. Neither the fund's dividend yield nor its returns are sky-high, but both are very strong for a fund with CARY's risk and volatility.

CARY's active management is something of a risk, and could always result in some underperformance, although that has not been the case in the past. The opposite is true, with the fund outperforming because of its active management, with decisions such as overweighting commercial MBS leading to incrementally higher returns.

CARY's below-average duration should lead to lower capital gains if rates plummet. I don't think the expected set of rate cuts qualifies, nor have these led to CARY's underperformance YTD, or during the past month (closer to the most recent cut).

CARY's outperformance was mostly due to the aforementioned successful active management.

Its overall outstanding results were made possible by past Federal Reserve hikes. CARY's portfolio might have yielded 3.0% during ZIRP, with fewer opportunities for alpha (interest rate volatility caused some serious bond market dislocations). During most of the past 15 years, the only way to get +5.0% in yield was with a lot of credit risk, interest rate risk, leverage, or some combination of the three.

Federal Reserve hikes have led to higher yields across the board, with high-quality securities offering respectable income, and lots of opportunities for alpha. CARY has taken advantage of these developments and created a simple, effective, high-quality portfolio with a good yield and solid returns.

On the flip side, Federal Reserve cuts should reduce CARY's yield and return moving forward, although it will somewhat depend on the speed and magnitude of any cuts. With a duration of 3.7 years, CARY's dividends should remain competitive for several years. Long-term, dividends should decrease, dependent on future Fed policy.

Conclusion

CARY's above-average 6.2% yield, strong performance track record, and below-average risk and volatility make the fund a buy.

data.title: Safestore: A Call Option On Self-Storage Momentum In Europe
data.description:
Introduction

Although I’m personally not a user of self-storage facilities, I understand the appeal of having the possibility to access these facilities. While it is very popular in North America, the entire concept is only slowly gaining ground in Europe, likely due to cultural differences. As it has been a while since I last discussed Safestore Holdings (OTCPK:SFSHF), I wanted to follow up on this name to see if there perhaps is an investment opportunity. The stock is up about 60% since my 2019 article. A pretty decent result despite the turmoil in the REIT sector earlier this year.

As the average volumes in the UK are substantially higher than in the U.S., I would strongly recommend you to trade in Safestore's shares through the facilities of the London Stock Exchange. The ticker symbol in London is SAFE, and the average daily volume is just over 300,000 shares.

A robust result for this self-storage operator

The REIT’s financial year ends in October, and the most recent detailed financial results are related to the first semester. As the income statement below will show, the REIT generated a total revenue of approximately 109M GBP, but if you look at the breakdown of what the reported revenue actually consists of, you see the total revenue from self-storage activities was just 91.6M GBP while the protection-related revenue was approximately 12M GBP.

Going back to the full income statement (shown below), the REIT reported an underlying EBITDA of 67.1M GBP and although the reported net income was approximately 157M GBP (representing an EPS of almost 72 pence per share in the first semester), I’d like to emphasize that just like in North America, the reported net income is a pretty useless metric for a REIT.

As you can see above, approximately 2/3 rd of the total net income came from a positive 121.7M GBP revaluation of investment properties during the first semester.

That’s why it makes sense to focus on the so-called EPRA earnings, which is a metric that’s pretty similar to the FFO calculation in North America. As you can see below, the starting point of the EPRA earnings is the almost 157M GBP in net profit, but the impact of the revaluation of the properties is isolated. This results in an adjusted profit of almost 49M GBP and after also deducting the impact of the change in the fair value of the lease liabilities, the adjusted EPRA earnings were 45M GBP. An additional 1.4M GBP correction was applied to take the impact of share-based payment charges into consideration.

As the image above shows, this resulted in an adjusted diluted EPRA EPS of 21.2 pence. The basic EPS was approximately 20.7 pence. On an annualized basis, this represents an underlying EPS of approximately 41.5 pence per share, applying a 75% occupancy.

That being said, Safestore is quite optimistic about its potential earnings growth. As you can see below, the REIT expects a negative earnings growth result in both 2024 and 2025, but anticipates lease renewals and the gradual completion of the development pipeline will start boosting the EPS from 2027 on. The Q3 trading update already showed a higher revenue and it will be interesting to see if Safestore will provide more color on its expectations for FY 2025 when it puts out a Q4 trading update in November.

As you can see above, the company anticipates a substantial EPS increase kicking off in 2027, and by 2030 the total impact should be north of 10 pence per share on an annualized basis. The starting point of the equation won’t be this year’s result, but is anticipated to be a 10.2 pence boost compared to the FY 2023 result of just under 47 pence. This means the implied EPRA earnings guidance for 2030 (or perhaps the subsequent year) comes in around 57 pence. And that includes the anticipated negative impact of the interest expenses going forward. The total required capex to achieve those growth numbers is approximately 170M GBP. As shown below, a substantial portion of the additional storage space will be created in the London and Paris areas. As shown below, approximately 1.15M square feet will be delivered in 2025 and 2026.

The focus on London and Paris isn’t a surprise as the average rent per square foot is for instance twice as high a sin the Netherlands (highlighted below).

So the earnings are resilient and the REIT is still growing; those are positive factors. But I, of course, need to have a look at the balance sheet again. Fortunately, British REITs have the tradition of maintaining a low leverage ratio, and Safestore isn’t any different. As of the end of the first semester, the REIT had an LTV ratio of just under 26% at an average effective interest rate of 3.77%.

The balance sheet contains 3.12B GBP in assets, with 2.84B GBP in investment properties and approximately 114M GBP in assets under construction. The cash position was almost 14M GBP. The current debt is 44M GBP with an additional 728M GBP in debt with staggered maturities, as you can see below.

As shown below, the properties were valued at an exit cap rate of approximately 5.4% and assuming a stabilized occupancy ratio of 89.4% (that’s higher than the current occupancy ratio).

Considering the risk-free interest rates are coming down, I’m moderately okay with using a 5.4% cap rate, but even if you’d use a 5.75% or 5.8% (self-storage facilities tend to have relatively low cap rates), the impact on the Net Tangible Assets per share would remain manageable. My bigger issue is with the assumption of an average occupancy ratio of close to 90% as Safestore is very far off that number with an average occupancy of around 75%.

As you can see below, the total Net Tangible Assets per share (using the EPRA guidance) is 1003 pence, and excluding the deferred tax liabilities on the revaluation of the properties, the net assets are 935 pence per share.

At the current share price of 843 pence, the stock is currently trading at a discount of approximately 16% to the EPRA NTA per share.

Investment thesis

Safestore is an interesting REIT as, although I’m not 100% convinced of the demand for self-storage space in Europe and considering the REIT’s current occupancy ratio is substantially below the assumed stabilized occupancy, the growth profile of this self-storage REIT remains interesting.

Given all these elements, Safestore may still be a ‘buy’ and a bet on the self-storage industry gaining ground in Europe. The dividend was 30.1 pence last year (subject to the 20% UK dividend withholding tax) and that’s an attractive yield for waiting for the earnings to increase.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Amazon Q3 Preview: Riding Nuclear To Record Profits
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Amazon's (NASDAQ:AMZN) stock drifted higher since my last analysis, up 14.42% since I last covered the company over the summer.

Amazon continues to fire on all cylinders, with growth continuing to benefit from a handful of strong tailwinds. We're really seeing this within Amazon Web Services (AWS) as well in their retail division, as US retail sales continue to power higher, reaching $714.4 billion in September - a 0.4% increase from the previous month and a 1.7% rise year-over-year​.

Consumer spending feels far more resilient now, and I expect more investor confidence in Amazon's retail operations as a result. This is not the biggest story going into earnings, however.

Amazon (as much as they are focused on growth) has become hyper-conscious on cost cuts. But these aren't the normal 'strip to the bone' cost cuts we have seen at other Fortune 500 companies in their pursuit of higher EPS. Rather, these are long-run optimizations that are set to make their business fundamentally more efficient (and I think this will be a long-run cost advantage).

Amazon's AWS has been looking to cut electricity costs as demand for data processing has exponentially increased due to the rise in demand for AI solutions.

Amazon is actively addressing these energy challenges by investing in the development of mini nuclear reactors to power their hyperscale data centers. Nuclear is increasingly becoming the preferred energy source. This has massive implications for data centers overall.

For Amazon, the $500 million investment ensures a stable and potentially lower-cost power supply. It comes at just the right time. Earlier this year, the company announced it will be spending $150 billion to build more data centers over the next 15 years.

With earnings around the corner, I think these investments are coming at just the right time. Amazon shares continue to be a strong buy as they invest more in the right types of innovation, and potentially increase the MOAT of AWS.

Why I'm Doing Follow-Up Coverage

Amazon continues to execute on many of the growth plans in AI that I outlined in my last piece back in August. As I noted in my previous research, Amazon is becoming one of the go-to places for companies to fine-tune their LLMs for their enterprise applications. It's helping Amazon offset the fact they do not have a leading frontier LLM. Their cloud unit recently booked a 19% increase in sales amid an uptick in AI-driven projects in Q2.

I think there's a lot of optimism surrounding AWS. But I think what is missing right now is how Amazon should (and looks like will) now prioritize cost management.

Electricity remains one of the single largest expenses for any data center, particularly with the increasing demand for computing power driven by AI applications. As I mentioned above, Amazon's initiative to invest in small modular reactors (SMRs) will help mitigate their energy costs. I think this will lead to long-run better margins as nuclear allows them to better control the largest variable data center cost. On top of this, the barrier to entry for nuclear will help Amazon make it harder for new industry entrants to reach the economies of scale they enjoy.

The purpose of this follow-up coverage is to show how Amazon is building a new type of competitive moat with nuclear.

Why Nuclear Is A Big Deal

The AI revolution is in full swing at hyperscalers like Amazon. Data centers (like the ones owned at AWS) are expected to see their power demand multiply due to the surge in AI demand (and therefore electricity to power the GPUs that will host the AI models).

Many of America's tech companies (now including Amazon) are investing in reliable carbon-free energy for their operations.

For Amazon, their investment agreements are specific. Amazon is investing in the development of nuclear energy around the deployment of multiple SMRs, which can be constructed and brought online faster than traditional nuclear reactors​. Their broad investment plan includes collaborations with Energy Northwest to develop four advanced SMRs that could provide up to 960 megawatts (MW) of capacity. This will likely generate an expected 320 MW of capacity for the first phase of the project, with the potential to raise it to 960 MW total to support 770,000 homes in the country.

By some estimates, nuclear power is about 1/5th the cost to produce compared to gas (natural gas) generated electricity. And while it's more expensive than renewable energy, it's also more stable (in terms of supply).

According to an interview with AWS CEO Matthew Garman:

We see the need for gigawatts of power in the coming years, and there's not going to be enough wind and solar projects to be able to meet the needs, and so nuclear is a great opportunity.

Also, the technology is really advancing to a place with SMRs where there's going to be a new technology that's going to be safe and that's going to be easy to manufacture in a much smaller form

- Matthew Garman, CEO of AWS.

On the company's official blog post, he also justified their decision:

Nuclear is a safe source of carbon-free energy that can help power our operations and meet the growing demands of our customers, while helping us progress toward our Climate Pledge commitment to be net-zero carbon across our operations by 2040…One of the fastest ways to address climate change is by transitioning our society to carbon-free energy sources, and nuclear energy is both carbon-free and able to scale-which is why it's an important area of investment for Amazon. Our agreements will encourage the construction of new nuclear technologies that will generate energy for decades to come.

Earnings Preview

Amazon is set to report its earnings on October 31st after the bell, with consensus estimates indicating an EPS of $1.14 for Q3, reflecting a year-over-year growth of approximately 20.93%​. Revenue is expected to come in at $157.27 billion, representing 9.91% growth.

While we are early into Amazon's nuclear journey, I'll be paying close attention to any nuclear mentions on the call.

Personally, given Amazon's recent announcements in nuclear, I expect management to set the stage by talking about how much they pay for energy within AWS, so investors see this energy cost as a benchmark going forward.

As the demand for power increases, particularly with the growth of AI and cloud services, this benchmark will feed into management's commentary on these nuclear projects and could provide us insights into their long-term cost control measures.

Personally, I think the market is already catching on (in part) to nuclear. EPS revision data for quarterly EPS tracking out into 2026 are starting to show a notable bump-up in EPS estimates over the last month. I think nuclear is helping power this (no pun intended).

Valuation

Amazon's current P/E is high when viewed through the lens of a traditional P/E ratio metric. The company currently sports a P/E of 40.17, well above the sector median of 17.16. To be clear, the market has rewarded Amazon with a forward P/E well above the sector median for years based on the expectation that forward revenue growth (and therefore EPS growth) would be strong. Now, based on cost efficiencies like going nuclear, I think the focus is going to shift from less on revenue powering a higher EPS, but now to EPS going up even as revenue growth slows based on efficiency gains.

Historically, Amazon has been underestimated on a PEG ratio. Their trailing 12-month PEG ratio of 0.2 is well below the sector median of 0.77. As a result, I think their forward PEG ratio should be about a 20% premium to the sector median to bake in the fact that the company will probably continue to beat expectations (now in part driven by nuclear) as a result.

The current forward PEG ratio is 1.81. If the forward PEG ratio were to increase by 20%, it would (obviously) imply a corresponding 20% increase in shares from here, and a PEG ratio of 2.17. Keep in mind, the median historical PEG ratio for Amazon is 3.12, so a 20% premium would not even mean the PEG ratio reaches the historical median.

Risks

I view Amazon's relentless push for automation as forward-thinking and how they continue to live in 'day-one' as founder Jeff Bezos describes his desire for the company to stay lean. However, in this case, nuclear power does not come without risks, particularly with lukewarm public perception and regulatory scrutiny surrounding the atomic energy form.

Nuclear energy remains a contentious issue here in the US. Historical incidents, such as the Three Mile Island accident in 1979, have left a lasting impression on public sentiment and brought real skepticism about the safety and viability of nuclear power.

Fortunately, for Amazon, public opinion is evolving. This evolution, in my opinion, may pave the way for greater acceptance of nuclear projects like the SMRs that Amazon is pursuing​.

Bottom Line

Amazon (and their stock for that matter) has continued to execute both YTD and since my last piece of analysis, driven by strong performance in both AWS and rising consumer spending that has given investors confidence that the e-commerce giant can continue to trend higher​.

Now, with their $500 million investment in nuclear energy to power their data centers, I think Amazon is taking the next necessary step in making their data centers far more efficient, lowering energy costs. Shares should benefit from this.

Nuclear energy presents a game-changing opportunity for Amazon's cost structure.

Current estimates indicate that nuclear power can be produced for about 1/5th the cost of natural gas. This should lead to even more savings and help improve profitability at AWS as the data center service scales up from the immense demand for AI.

While some investors may be concerned that investments in nuclear energy could face the same negative public reception as the infamous Three Mile Island incident did in the US, I think the public in the US is now far more accepting of nuclear energy. We all know the effects of a warming planet (and also how unstable renewable energy can be). Amazon sees this too.

With this, I think shares continue to be a strong buy. Amazon continues to execute.

data.title: CAVA Group: Don't Fight The Momentum
data.description:
Overview

I previously covered CAVA Group (NYSE:CAVA) back in August 2023 and issued a buy rating. Since then, the price has grown by a staggering amount greater than 200%, severely outpacing the S&P 500's return. On a YTD basis alone, CAVA has increased by 217%, which has been driven by a rapid expansion rate, healthy financial margins, and a very strategic approach to their business model. After my first article, I realized that investors were downplaying this company because they were looking at it from a surface level perspective. The food products they offer aren't new, but the business practices are uniquely strong. While investors may make comparisons to Chipotle (CMG), CAVA stands because of its capital efficiency and the level of revenue growth achieved with fewer locations.

Although I am primarily a dividend focused investor, I sprinkle in some growth oriented holdings when I see an extremely attractive opportunity. Despite the massive run that CAVA had, I believe that there is still upside growth that can be captured. The natural psychology of investors would be to shy away from adding or entering a position near all-time highs, and that's totally understandable in most cases. However, I do not believe that this price run is unwarranted as CAVA has consistently increased earnings at a high rate of growth while maintaining high profitability margins to increase same store sales.

For context, CAVA operates as a fast casual restaurant with plenty of locations spread across the US. CAVA's IPO happened in June 2023 and has taken off since then. The business differentiates itself through its Mediterranean centric cuisine, with an emphasis on a diverse selection of foods. Let's first start by reviewing the company's financial strength and why the positive momentum has been strong.

Financial Strength

CAVA last reported their Q2 earnings around the end of August, the results were strong. For instance, earnings per share came in at $0.17, a sizeable increase from an EPS of $0.13 from the prior Q1. Additionally, revenue for Q2 landed at $231M, representing a massive year-over-year growth of over 35%. A lot of this growth comes from an improvement in same restaurant sales growth, which landed at 14.4%. As CAVA sees more foot traffic, these numbers will continue to improve until restaurant growth drastically slows down. More foot traffic is naturally associated with the continued openings of new locations throughout the country.

Speaking of restaurant count, there are now 341 different locations across the US. The large majority of these locations span from the northeast of the US and follows the coast down to Florida. The only source of weakness would be that CAVA lacks a meaningful presence on the west coast of the US and serves as an opportunity for additional expansion efforts at its own pace. While they do have a tiny presence in the California and Texas markets, there are still plenty more opportunities for growth throughout the rest of the West Coast. They made their entry into the middle of the US with their first Chicago location opening up earlier this year.

Not to mention, CAVA currently has zero international exposure at the moment. Therefore, the opportunity for future growth is absolutely huge and since the company is so well-managed, I do not see any red flags that would serve as a barrier to continued growth at the moment. Just for comparison, Chipotle currently has over 3,300 locations open and has only averaged a 15.29% year-over-year revenue growth over the last five years.

A lot of the strength of this company is that it is well managed through capital efficiency and doesn't take on a lot of debt to fuel these expansion efforts. It helps that they operate with a healthy profit margin of 26.5%, which is a new high over the last year. I dug through the most recent form 10-Q and discovered that the company operates on almost zero debt at the moment. While CAVA has an available borrowing capacity of $74.3M through different revolving loan commitments, they own hold $700k worth of outstanding letters of credit. The outstanding balance originated in 2022 and has a five-year maturity out until 2027. So while CAVA could technically fuel expansion much faster by taking on debt, I prefer this slower and more calculated approach that leaves them less vulnerable to macro shifts in the economy, such as interest rate fluctuations.

The financial strength is further reinforced by a growing cash and equivalents total, which now sits as high as $343.7M. This is the largest cash balance to date and can be used to head the company to navigate through unexpected headwinds or contribute to additional expansion efforts. Free cash flow levels now sit at $22.7M for the quarter, a major improvement from the prior year's Q2 total of ($11.9M). Lastly, the adjusted EBITDA currently sits at $34.3M. I believe we will see the range of EBITDA stabilize to a smaller and more consistent price range once economic conditions improve for the average consumer.

Improving Economic Indicators

A lot of the caution around a consumer reliant stock such as CAVA is directly related to what the economic environment looks like. If consumer spending levels decrease, this obviously has a negative impact of CAVA's sales growth. One of the main drivers of consumer spending levels would be the rate of inflation. Over the last couple of years, inflation levels have remained elevated, reaching as high as 9.1% around the middle of 2022. Consumers felt the pressure of the increased costs of living and this created some pessimism around the success of a company like CAVA.

However, I believe that conditions are starting to improve and this serves as a positive growth catalyst. The Fed is targeting an inflation level of 2%, and the latest September report shows that inflation now sits at 2.4%. The rate of inflation has now decreased for six consecutive months in a row and trends towards that targeted 2% level. As inflation levels decrease, consumers will see a bit of relief and this should boost spending levels and free how the amount of disposable income that households have.

Similarly, we have the potential for interest rate cuts to serve as another catalyst for price growth. Despite the business currently holding close to no debt relative to its size, a lower interest rate reduces the future cost of borrowing in case CAVA ever wants to fuel growth at a more rapid pace. Additionally, lower interest rates can also boost consumer spending as lower mortgage payments, car loan payments, and credit card interest payments provide some relief and increase disposable income levels. If CAVA plans to continue their growth momentum, lower interest rates would have a positive impact of the appeal of acquiring new commercial real estate as well. It's a trickle effect that would ultimately be positive for future valuation growth.

The Fed already implemented their first interest rate cut of 50 basis points in September. I anticipate additional interest rate cuts happening over the next twelve months, driven by an increase in the unemployment rate, upcoming US Presidential elections, and higher inflation. The unemployment rate has steadily increased over the last twelve months and now sits at 4.1%. Higher inflation usually correlates to stricter consumer spending as households try to cut back on non-necessities.

Additionally, the US Presidential elections usually create a market environment of higher volatility and uncertainty. We will shortly find out what the market sentiment looks like after the results of elections. I believe the combination of these factors will incentivize the Fed to continue cutting interest rates as a way to provide some stimulus.

Calculated Approach

Data compiled by Placer shows that CAVA has a very strategic approach to their expansion plan by focusing on areas where the median household income is higher than normal. While this isn't a foolproof strategy, it does limit some of the vulnerability to a decreased foot traffic during rough economic times. Higher income households usually show more resilience to recessions and other headwinds, so they are more likely to maintain a level of disposable income for the pleasantries like a meal to eat. Even if there are drops in traffic, CAVA can offset this with marginal price increases to a consumer base that would be more likely to be able to afford it.

This isn't a unique strategy by any means and reminds me of the approach that Trader Joe's uses for their store openings. Trader Joe's opens their locations in areas where the median household income is 10% greater than the average. This is exactly what other public business like Starbucks (SBUX) also does to help mitigate some of the consumer-facing vulnerabilities. SBUX also aims to open locations near wealthier income households. The only difference is that CAVA implements this strategy without the crushing weight of debt to fuel their new restaurant openings.

We can see that nationwide, CAVA has locations in the proximity of households with a median income of $91K, which is 20% higher than the nation's average household income. Despite not having a lot of locations in California, we can see that they've been able to maintain a location proximity close to households with a median income of $114.9K, which is 22% above the state's median household income.

The current guidance for CAVA also looks strong and will add to the positive momentum. They expect to open more stores than previously, now amounting to a range between 54 and 57 new openings. Same restaurant sales are now forecasted to grow between 8.5% to 9.5%, a sizeable improvement over the prior guidance. This is all while profit margins are estimated to slightly improve while pre-opening costs remain exactly the same. There isn't a single downside to the estimated guidance outlook and this strength will contribute to continued success. I don't think that investors should fight the momentum at the moment.

Risk Profile

As the NASDAQ and S&P 500 continually close near their all-time highs, valuations can seem a bit rich at the moment. As a defensive play, investors may start to take some of their gains and cause valuations to slightly decrease. While we don't have a crystal ball to tell the future, this is always an ongoing threat as the indexes remain near their tops. CAVA has run up over 210% since its public inception, without much of a break in momentum. It can always be scary adding near the top, so buying interest can be a suppressed at the moment.

If CAVA's growth momentum of same store restaurant sales and new store openings starts to slow, investors may see this as a sign of weakness and the outlook may soften. This may result in a sell-off and drive the price downward. There were recently reports that suggest insiders could be selling their shares, which is usually an indication that investors will follow suite. Investors may see this as insiders taking profits before a weak quarter is announced, but they could be selling shares for the simple sake of profit taking. We won't know until the next earnings report is announced.

Artal International filed to sell 6 million shares, while CEO Brett Schulman filed to sell 201,504 shares. Other executives filed to sell smaller blocks of shares.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on CAVA as the growth of the business is still on a strong upward momentum. The business is retaining more free cash flow, growing the strength of their balance sheet by avoiding debt, and expanding same restaurant sales at a high capacity. The forecasted guidance is strong and was revised to show improvements in sales growth, operating profit margin, and new restaurant openings while simultaneously keeping opening costs the same. Improvements related to decreases in inflation and future interest rate cuts could also serve as a positive price catalyst for future growth. CAVA takes a very strategic approach to their business by focusing on areas that have a household income higher than the median, which helps mitigate the vulnerability to decrease in consumer traffic.
